_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Pattnaik 2006 (KFD: a review, WHO/Bulletin), Dodd et al. on the 2023-24 outbreaks in Karnataka and Kerala. Clinical descriptions from work by Bhat et al. and TRC (Talchery Research Centre) KFD surveillance. Vaccine data from the KFDV inactivated vaccine trials and Indian Council of Medical Research post-marketing studies. Transmission from Worken et al. on Haemaphysalis spinigera ecology.

h1Title: Kyasanur Forest Disease: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Kyasanur Forest disease (KFD) is a tick-borne viral haemorrhagic fever caused by Kyasanur Forest disease virus (KFDV), a flavivirus in the Nairoviridae family transmitted by Haemaphysalis spinigera ticks. First identified in 1957 after an epizootic among langur and bonnet macaque monkeys in Karnataka, India, KFD causes biphasic fever with haemorrhagic manifestations, severe myalgia, and prostration. The disease is endemic to the western ghats forest regions of Karnataka and has spread to other states including Kerala, Maharashtra, Goa, and Tamil Nadu. The overall case fatality rate is 3-10% in hospitalised patients. Diagnosis is by reverse transcription PCR or ELISA, and treatment is supportive as no specific antiviral exists. An inactivated tissue culture vaccine (KFDV vaccine) is available and recommended for at-risk populations in endemic areas, with 70-80% efficacy against laboratory-confirmed infection.

keyStats.prevalence: Since 1957, annual human cases range from 100 to 500 in Karnataka alone; larger outbreaks (2023-24) have seen over 1,000 suspected cases in a single season (Dodd et al. 2024, preprint)
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 20-60 most affected; predominantly rural populations entering forested areas; forest workers, farmers, and tribal communities bear the highest burden; no significant sex predilection in adults
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of reported cases 35-45 years; children are affected in focal outbreaks near villages
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic to Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Goa, and Tamil Nadu states of India; no documented transmission outside India to date; estimated 1,000-2,000 cases annually across all affected states

definition: Kyasanur Forest disease (ICD-10: A98.2) is a zoonotic viral haemorrhagic fever caused by Kyasanur Forest disease virus (KFDV), a flavivirus of the genus Orthonairovirus, family Nairoviridae. The virus is maintained in nature by a tick-mammal cycle involving Haemaphysalis spinigera as the primary vector and small mammals (rodents, shrews) as amplifying hosts. Monkeys (Semnopithecus entellus and Macaca radiata) develop severe disease and serve as sentinels, with monkey deaths often preceding human outbreaks. Humans acquire infection through the bite of infected ticks during forest activities or through contact with infected animal tissues. KFD has an incubation period of 3-8 days, followed by a biphasic illness: an initial viraemic phase with high fever, severe headache, myalgia, and gastrointestinal symptoms lasting 1-2 weeks, and (in severe cases) a haemorrhagic phase with ecchymoses, gingival bleeding, epistaxis, and haematemesis. The disease has a case fatality rate of 3-10% in hospitalised patients, with deaths occurring during the haemorrhagic phase from multi-organ involvement. Diagnosis is confirmed by RT-PCR in the first week of illness or by IgM ELISA in the second week. Treatment is supportive as no specific antiviral is available. An inactivated KFDV vaccine administered in two doses 4-6 weeks apart provides 70-80% protection and is distributed in endemic areas during pre-outbreak campaigns. KFD is managed by infectious disease specialists, often with tropical medicine and critical care input in severe cases.

typesClassification.type: Mild KFD (febrile phase without haemorrhagic progression)
typesClassification.description: The majority of infections present with fever, headache, and myalgia without progression to the haemorrhagic phase. Symptoms resolve within 1-2 weeks with supportive care. These patients have a low fatality rate but may be under-reported.

typesClassification.type: Moderate KFD (haemorrhagic phase with controlled bleeding)
typesClassification.description: Progression to the haemorrhagic phase occurs in a subset of patients, with petechiae, ecchymoses, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, and haematemesis. With close monitoring and supportive care (blood products, fluid management), survival rates improve significantly.

A minority of patients develop severe multi-organ involvement with hepatic failure, coagulopathy, renal impairment, and shock. Mortality is highest in this group. Requires ICU-level care with organ support.

Rarely, KFD causes meningoencephalitis or other neurological sequelae during or after the acute phase. These patients require neurology input and prolonged follow-up for potential long-term cognitive or motor deficits.

primarySymptoms: High fever (38.5-41 C) of abrupt onset, often with rigors, typically lasting 1-2 weeks in the initial phase.
Severe myalgia and body ache, particularly affecting the back and legs, causing difficulty in movement and prostration.
Intense retro-orbital headache and photophobia in the first 2-5 days of illness.
Nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea in the first week, contributing to dehydration and weakness.
Haemorrhagic manifestations in the second week: petechiae, ecchymoses, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, and gastrointestinal bleeding.
Biphasic pattern: initial improvement followed by recrudescence of fever and haemorrhagic manifestations.
Severe fatigue and prostration that persists for weeks after the acute phase.
Cough and respiratory symptoms in a subset of patients.
Lymphadenopathy and relative bradycardia during the febrile phase.
Hepatomegaly and splenomegaly in moderate to severe disease.
Patients with severe disease may develop jaundice, melaena, haematemesis, and signs of shock.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of high fever with severe myalgia in a patient with recent forest exposure in Karnataka or neighbouring states during the KFD season (November-June)
Haemorrhagic skin manifestations (petechiae, bruising) appearing on the second week of illness
Severe headache with vomiting in a febrile patient from an endemic area
Monkey deaths reported in the same village or forest area within the preceding 2-4 weeks
Rapid clinical deterioration with falling platelet count and rising liver enzymes in a febrile patient
emergencySigns: Haematemesis, melaena, or gross gastrointestinal bleeding with haemodynamic instability — requires immediate transfusion and ICU monitoring
Spontaneous intracranial haemorrhage or signs of CNS bleeding (severe headache, focal deficits, altered consciousness) — requires urgent neuroimaging and neurosurgical input
Hypovolaemic shock from massive haemorrhage with tachycardia, hypotension, and altered mental status — requires aggressive fluid resuscitation and blood product support
Acute hepatic failure with encephalopathy (jaundice, asterixis, confusion) — requires specialist hepatology input and consideration of liver support
Respiratory failure with hypoxia from pulmonary haemorrhage — requires ICU-level respiratory support

causes.cause: Bite of infected Haemaphysalis spinigera ticks
causes.description: H. spinigera is the primary vector of KFDV in nature. Nymph and adult ticks acquire the virus by feeding on viraemic small mammals (rodents, shrews) during the amplification phase. Humans acquire infection during forest activities when infected ticks bite and transmit the virus.

Forest workers, farmers, and tribal communities who handle dead monkeys or rodents during outbreaks can acquire infection through broken skin or mucous membrane contact with infectious tissues. Monkey carcasses are highly infectious during the acute phase.

causes.cause: Tick exposure during forest activities in endemic areas
causes.description: Entering forested areas of the western ghats during the transmission season (November to June, peaking January-March) without protective clothing or tick checks significantly raises risk. Hunting, fishing, wood collection, and grazing activities are documented risk activities.

causes.cause: Viraemic monkey as amplifying host and sentinel
causes.description: Monkey species Semnopithecus entellus (Hanuman langur) and Macaca radiata (bonnet macaque) develop severe viraemia and die during KFD outbreaks, serving as sentinels for human disease. Monkey deaths often precede human cases by 1-2 weeks.

causes.cause: Rodent reservoir mammals maintaining virus in nature
causes.description: Small mammals including rats (Rattus rattus), field mice, and shrews (Suncus murinus) maintain KFDV in nature through tick-mammal cycles without becoming severely ill. These are the natural amplifying hosts between tick generations.

causes.cause: Lack of vaccination in at-risk populations
causes.description: The inactivated KFDV vaccine is not universally used; coverage gaps in endemic villages and new migrant workers mean many at-risk individuals are unvaccinated and fully susceptible to KFDV infection.

riskFactors.factor: Forest work in endemic districts of Karnataka during transmission season (Nov-Jun)
riskFactors.description: Farmers, hunters, gatherers, and daily wage workers entering the western ghats forest during the KFD season have the highest risk. The peak season (January-March) coincides with highest tick activity.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with dead monkeys or rodents
riskFactors.description: Forest department workers, villagers, and tribal communities who handle animal carcasses during die-offs are at very high risk of KFDV transmission through skin or mucous membrane exposure.
Unvaccinated residents of endemic villages and arriving workers have no protection against KFDV. Two-dose vaccine coverage in endemic villages is targeted at 80-90% but remains incomplete.
Not using permethrin-treated clothing, not performing daily tick checks, not using DEET-based repellents, and wearing open footwear in forested areas increase tick bite risk.

riskFactors.factor: Living or working in proximity to primate populations
riskFactors.description: Villages adjacent to monkey-inhabited forest areas have higher risk during epizootic periods when monkey deaths signal increased virus circulation in ticks.
Patients with chronic liver disease, coagulation disorders, or immunosuppression are more likely to progress to the haemorrhagic phase and have worse outcomes.

riskFactors.factor: Delay in seeking hospital care during haemorrhagic phase
riskFactors.description: Patients from remote forest villages who present late in the disease course have higher mortality due to delays in supportive care and blood product transfusion.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-60 engaged in forest activities (farming, hunting, wood collection) in endemic districts of Karnataka — particularly Uttar Kannada, Shimoga, Dakshina Kannada, and Udupi districts.
Tribal communities and forest-dwelling populations in the western ghats region with high exposure to tick-infested forest environments.
Forest department workers and wildlife researchers handling animal carcasses during KFD die-offs have documented high seroconversion rates.
Seasonal workers and migrants entering endemic forest areas for agricultural labour during the October-June transmission season.
Children in endemic villages account for a growing proportion of reported cases as outbreaks have expanded geographically.
diagnosisOverview: KFD should be suspected in any patient with acute onset high fever, severe myalgia, and haemorrhagic manifestations who has been in a KFD-endemic area within 2 weeks, particularly during the November-June transmission season when monkey deaths have been reported. Early diagnosis by RT-PCR from whole blood or serum (collected in the first 5-7 days of illness) is critical for outbreak containment and case management. KFDV RNA is detectable by nested RT-PCR or real-time RT-PCR with high sensitivity and specificity. IgM ELISA becomes positive from day 5-7 of illness and persists for 2-4 months; it is useful for diagnosis in the second week and for surveillance. Virus isolation in cell culture (BHK-21 or Vero cells) is available at reference laboratories (National Institute of Virology, Pune). Routine laboratory findings include leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated aspartate aminotransferase and alanine aminotransferase, and prolonged prothrombin time in severe disease. Differential diagnosis includes dengue fever, chikungunya, scrub typhus, leptospirosis, malaria, Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever (also tick-borne in India), and other viral haemorrhagic fevers. Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever is the most important differential in western India as both present with biphasic haemorrhagic fever; tick exposure history and specific serology distinguish them.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Early confirmatory diagnosis in the first week of illness; detects viral RNA in serum or whole blood
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood collected in EDTA tube; results in 24-48 hours at reference labs. Sensitivity is highest on days 1-5 of illness. Turnaround can be delayed in remote endemic areas.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Alternative nucleic acid detection with high sensitivity and specificity; used in reference and research laboratories
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same sample as RT-PCR; results in 24-72 hours. More sensitive than real-time RT-PCR in some evaluations but requires skilled laboratory personnel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serological diagnosis from day 5-7 of illness; detects virus-specific IgM indicating recent or acute infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 1-3 days. Sensitivity increases after day 7. Persists for weeks to months, useful for surveillance and retrospective diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects past infection or seroconversion after vaccination; useful for seroprevalence studies in endemic areas
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 1-3 days. IgG appears after IgM and persists for years. Confirmed seroconversion (4-fold rise or seroconversion in paired samples) is diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard confirmatory test; isolates live KFDV from patient blood in BHK-21 or Vero cells
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialist BSL-3 laboratory only. Results in 5-10 days. Reserved for reference and research settings; not available clinically.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with platelet count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Monitors for thrombocytopenia and leukopenia, hallmarks of KFD progression to haemorrhagic phase
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours. Rapidly declining platelets below 50,000/cu mm signal progression risk and guide transfusion threshold.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests (AST, ALT, bilirubin, albumin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses hepatic involvement; markedly elevated AST/ALT and falling albumin indicate severe disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours. Serial testing guides prognosis and liver support needs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates coagulopathy; prolonged PT and low fibrinogen with high D-dimer indicate disseminated intravascular coagulation in severe KFD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results within hours. Guides fresh frozen plasma, cryoprecipitate, and platelet transfusion thresholds.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of KFD is entirely supportive as no specific antiviral therapy is available. Early recognition and prompt supportive care significantly reduce mortality. Fluid management with crystalloid solutions maintains hydration; care is needed in severe hepatic involvement where fluid overload risk rises. Antipyretics (paracetamol 500-1000 mg every 6 hours) manage fever and discomfort; NSAIDs should be avoided due to bleeding risk from platelet dysfunction. Analgesics for severe myalgia (paracetamol, tramadol) are used as needed. Thrombocytopenia and coagulopathy are managed with blood product transfusion when clinically significant bleeding occurs or platelet counts fall below 20,000/cu mm. Fresh frozen plasma is used for active bleeding with prolonged PT; cryoprecipitate for hypofibrinogenaemia; and platelet concentrates for severe thrombocytopenia with bleeding. In severe cases with multi-organ involvement, ICU-level care including vasopressor support for shock, mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure, and renal replacement therapy for acute kidney injury is required. Corticosteroids have no proven benefit and are not recommended. Ribavirin and favipiravir have been tested in vitro and in animal models but clinical data in humans are lacking; they are not currently standard of care. The KFDV inactivated vaccine (2 doses 4-6 weeks apart, annual boosters) is effective for pre-exposure prophylaxis and is deployed in endemic areas as a public health intervention. The WHO has not listed KFD as a target for travel restrictions, but travellers to endemic areas during the transmission season should receive the vaccine if their visit will involve prolonged forest exposure.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive fluid management (crystalloid IV fluids)
medicalTreatments.description: Maintains intravascular volume and perfusion during the viraemic and haemorrhagic phases. Use caution in hepatic failure where sodium and fluid overload risk rises.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Critical for preventing hypovolaemic shock in patients with haemorrhagic manifestations; reduces mortality when instituted early

medicalTreatments.name: Antipyretic therapy (paracetamol 500-1000 mg orally every 6 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Controls high fever and reduces rigors; reduces metabolic demand and improves patient comfort.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for fever control; does not affect disease course but improves symptom management

medicalTreatments.name: Blood product transfusion (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate)
medicalTreatments.description: Guided by platelet count and coagulation parameters; transfuse for active bleeding or platelets below 20,000/cu mm. Fresh frozen plasma for prolonged PT with bleeding; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen below 100 mg/dL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significantly reduces mortality from haemorrhagic complications; requires close monitoring of transfusion triggers

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesia (paracetamol, tramadol 50-100 mg orally every 6-8 hours as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Manages severe myalgia and headache that cause significant distress. Opioids are rarely needed but appropriate for severe pain in monitored settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for pain control; avoid NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen) due to platelet dysfunction and bleeding risk

medicalTreatments.name: Vasopressor support (noradrenaline, vasopressin) for refractory shock
medicalTreatments.description: Initiated in ICU for distributive shock unresponsive to fluid resuscitation. Requires arterial line monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Life-saving in refractory shock; improves perfusion to critical organs

medicalTreatments.name: ICU organ support (mechanical ventilation, RRT for acute kidney injury)
medicalTreatments.description: Severe KFD with respiratory failure, hepatic failure, or acute kidney injury requires ICU-level care. Mechanical ventilation for pulmonary haemorrhage or encephalopathy; CRRT or haemodialysis for renal failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality in severe multi-organ KFD; outcomes depend on early ICU transfer and availability of advanced support

alternativeTreatments.name: KFDV inactivated vaccine (2 doses, 4-6 weeks apart, annual booster)
alternativeTreatments.description: Formalin-inactivated tissue culture vaccine provides 70-80% protection against laboratory-confirmed KFD. Pre-exposure prophylaxis is the primary preventive tool in endemic areas; deployed by state health departments before and during transmission season.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ribavirin (in vitro and limited clinical data, not standard of care)
alternativeTreatments.description: Ribavirin has shown in vitro activity against KFDV and has been used in related hemorrhagic fevers (Crimean-Congo). Clinical data in KFD are lacking; it is not currently recommended as standard care.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist is required to confirm KFD by RT-PCR or IgM ELISA, manage the biphasic disease course, monitor for progression to the haemorrhagic phase, and provide critical care coordination in severe cases. Early specialist involvement also enables public health notification and outbreak containment measures.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek care at a hospital with infectious disease, critical care, and laboratory capacity to perform RT-PCR or ELISA for KFDV. District hospitals in Karnataka are trained for KFD management. For severe cases, transfer to a tertiary centre with ICU capacity is essential. Ask about the hospital's KFD protocol, blood product availability, and ICU bed capacity.
hospitalCriteria: Infectious disease specialist or trained physician with KFD protocol
RT-PCR and IgM ELISA laboratory capability (regional reference laboratory network in Karnataka)
Blood bank with platelets, fresh frozen plasma, and cryoprecipitate
ICU with mechanical ventilation and vasopressor capability
Referral pathway to NIV Pune for virus isolation and specialist confirmatory testing
keyFacilities: District hospitals in KFD-endemic districts of Karnataka (Uttara Kannada, Shimoga, Dakshina Kannada, Udupi)
Karnataka State Referral Infectious Diseases Hospital
National Institute of Virology (NIV), Pune — reference laboratory for KFDV
Tertiary care centres with ICU and hepatology support in Bangalore and Manipal
KFD treatment wards established in endemic districts during outbreak season
preventionStrategies: Apply DEET-based tick repellent (DEET 30-50%) on exposed skin and permethrin on clothing before entering tick-infested forest areas in KFD-endemic districts.
Wear long-sleeved shirts, trousers tucked into socks, and closed shoes when entering forest; light-coloured clothing makes ticks easier to spot.
Perform daily whole-body tick checks after forest activities and remove attached ticks promptly with tweezers or a tick removal tool.
Obtain the KFDV inactivated vaccine (2 doses 4-6 weeks apart, annual booster) if living or working in an endemic area; free through state health department campaigns.
Avoid handling dead monkeys or rodents in KFD-endemic areas; notify forest department if unusual monkey deaths are observed.
Apply permethrin-treated clothing for forest workers and regularly scheduled mass prevention campaigns in endemic villages.
lifestyleModifications: Stay on marked trails and avoid brushing against vegetation in forest areas where Haemaphysalis ticks are most active.
Bathe and perform a full body tick check (including scalp, groin, armpits) after any forest activity in KFD-endemic areas.
If a tick is found attached, remove it immediately with fine-tipped tweezers, grasping close to the skin, and clean the bite site with antiseptic.
Sleep in protected accommodation (bed nets, closed rooms) when staying overnight in forest areas during KFD season.
Report any unexplained fever within 2 weeks of forest exposure to the nearest health centre and mention the forest exposure history.
If monkey deaths are reported in or near your village, avoid that area until health department authorities confirm it is safe.
Vaccinated individuals should still perform tick avoidance; the vaccine is approximately 70-80% effective.
Forest workers should receive annual KFDV vaccine boosters and use permethrin-treated work clothing.

recommended: High-calorie, soft diet during the acute phase when nausea, vomiting, and sore throat reduce oral intake
Frequent small meals and oral rehydration solutions to maintain hydration when gastrointestinal symptoms are present
Protein-rich diet (eggs, fish, chicken, legumes) during recovery to restore muscle mass lost during the febrile phase
Fresh fruit juices rich in vitamin C (orange, mosambi) to support immune function and reduce oxidative stress
avoid: NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen) and antiplatelet agents due to bleeding risk from thrombocytopenia
Alcohol during acute illness and recovery due to hepatotoxicity risk and impaired immune response
Raw or undercooked food from uncertain sources during the acute phase; risk of secondary bacterial gastroenteritis
Heavy, oily, or spicy meals during the gastrointestinal phase when nausea and vomiting are prominent

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute febrile and haemorrhagic phases, complete rest is required. Patients should not engage in physical activity. Graduated return to normal activity begins 2-4 weeks after fever resolution, guided by residual fatigue and any organ involvement. Full recovery, including resolution of myalgia and fatigue, may take 4-8 weeks. Light activity such as walking is permitted once fever has resolved and the patient is haemodynamically stable.
dailyManagement: Isolate during the viraemic phase to prevent potential secondary transmission via tick bites (unlikely human-to-human but precautions reduce environmental exposure).
Take antipyretics (paracetamol) every 6 hours for fever and severe headache; record temperature every 4 hours.
Monitor for bleeding signs (easy bruising, gum bleeding, blood in urine or stool) and report immediately to medical staff.
Maintain strict bed rest during the haemorrhagic phase; do not stand or walk unassisted if platelet count is below 50,000/cu mm.
Continue oral rehydration and small frequent meals; do not force food if nauseated.
Complete the full course of any prescribed medication; inform the medical team of any new symptoms such as worsening headache, confusion, or reduced urine output.
After discharge, rest at home for 2-4 weeks; avoid strenuous activity, swimming, or forest activities until fully recovered.
prognosis: Most KFD patients make a full recovery with supportive care; mortality is 3-10% in hospitalised cases and much lower in patients with mild disease who receive early care. Deaths occur in the haemorrhagic phase due to massive bleeding, multi-organ failure, or secondary infections. Survivors of severe KFD recover over 4-8 weeks with resolution of fever, bleeding, and laboratory abnormalities. Long-term sequelae are uncommon but include persistent fatigue, chronic hepatitis, and (rarely) neurological deficits. The KFDV vaccine reduces the risk of laboratory-confirmed infection by 70-80% and substantially reduces severe disease and death in vaccinated individuals who acquire infection. Early presentation and hospital admission significantly improve outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and viraemic phase last 1-2 weeks in uncomplicated cases. Haemorrhagic manifestations typically resolve within 1-2 weeks of onset with supportive care. Thrombocytopenia and elevated liver enzymes normalise over 3-6 weeks in survivors. Full return to baseline activity typically takes 4-8 weeks. Severe cases requiring ICU care may have prolonged recovery of 2-3 months.
complications: Multi-organ haemorrhagic syndrome with disseminated intravascular coagulation — the primary cause of death in severe KFD; requires ICU management with blood products and organ support
Hepatic failure with jaundice and coagulopathy — may require specialist hepatology input and carries high mortality
Acute kidney injury from hypotension, rhabdomyolysis, or direct viral injury — may require renal replacement therapy
Secondary bacterial infections (pneumonia, sepsis) in severely ill patients with prolonged ICU stays
Pulmonary haemorrhage causing respiratory failure — managed with mechanical ventilation and blood product support
Long-term chronic fatigue syndrome and asthenia persisting for months after acute infection in some survivors

supportResources.name: WHO — Kyasanur Forest disease fact sheet
supportResources.description: World Health Organization disease fact sheet covering KFD epidemiology, transmission, prevention, and WHO guidance on surveillance and control.

supportResources.name: CDC — Kyasanur Forest disease health information for travellers
supportResources.description: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention information on KFD for travellers and healthcare providers.

supportResources.name: National Institute of Virology (NIV), Pune — KFD diagnostic services
supportResources.description: India's premier viral research institute providing reference diagnostics for KFDV and training for state health departments.

supportResources.name: Karnataka Department of Health and Family Welfare — KFD outbreak updates
supportResources.description: Official Karnataka state government health portal with current KFD outbreak data, vaccination schedules, and district health contact information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue haemorrhagic fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue is transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes in tropical urban settings and presents with fever, retro-orbital pain, rash, and haemorrhagic manifestations. KFD involves forest exposure, Haemaphysalis tick bite, severe myalgia, and monkey sentinel deaths in the western ghats. RT-PCR and IgM ELISA for KFDV vs. dengue NS1 antigen/serology differentiate them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever (CCHF)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CCHF is transmitted by Hyalomma ticks in South Asia and presents with similar biphasic haemorrhagic fever. CCHF is caused by a Nairovirus (different genus) and has a higher fatality rate (5-30%). Epidemiological history (forest vs. livestock exposure), tick vector identification, and specific RT-PCR or ELISA distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scrub typhus (Orientia tsutsugamushi)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scrub typhus is transmitted by chigger mites (Leptotrombidium) in rural overgrown areas and presents with fever, eschar, lymphadenopathy, and rash. KFD presents with more prominent haemorrhagic manifestations, severe myalgia, and forest tick exposure. Scrub typhus responds to doxycycline; KFD does not. Specific serology differentiates.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is transmitted through contact with water or soil contaminated by rodent urine and presents with fever, myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, and jaundice. KFD involves forest tick exposure, haemorrhagic manifestations, and monkey deaths. Dark-field microscopy of blood for spirochetes and MAT serology for Leptospira differentiate it from KFD.

relatedConditions.name: Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever
relatedConditions.relevance: KFD and CCHF are both tick-borne viral haemorrhagic fevers in South Asia with overlapping clinical presentations. CCHF occurs in Gujarat, Rajasthan, and other states with Hyalomma tick exposure. Both require biosafety-level laboratory diagnosis and similar supportive care.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue haemorrhagic fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-circulating in Karnataka during the same season; both cause fever, haemorrhagic manifestations, and thrombocytopenia. Different vectors (Aedes vs. tick), different specific therapies (none for either), and different prevention strategies distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Scrub typhus
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic in parts of Karnataka; transmitted by chigger mites, treated with doxycycline, and presents with fever and eschar. KFD lacks the characteristic eschar and does not respond to doxycycline. Co-infections have been documented.

relatedConditions.name: Tick-borne relapsing fever (Borrelia species)
relatedConditions.relevance: Tick-borne relapsing fever causes recurrent fever, myalgia, and headache from Borrelia transmitted by Ornithodoros ticks. Distinguished by relapsing fever pattern and Borrelia blood smear; KFD is a single-biphasic illness confirmed by KFDV serology.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria (P. falciparum)
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic in Karnataka; presents with fever, chills, anaemia, and (in severe cases) jaundice and bleeding. Thick and thin blood films for Plasmodium and rapid antigen tests differentiate malaria from KFD.

faqs.question: What is Kyasanur Forest disease?
faqs.answer: Kyasanur Forest disease (KFD) is a tick-borne viral haemorrhagic fever caused by Kyasanur Forest disease virus, a flavivirus. It was first identified in Karnataka, India, in 1957 and causes biphasic fever with haemorrhagic manifestations and severe myalgia.

faqs.question: What causes KFD?
faqs.answer: KFD is caused by Kyasanur Forest disease virus (KFDV), a flavivirus in the Nairoviridae family, transmitted by the bite of Haemaphysalis spinigera ticks in forest areas of Karnataka, India.

faqs.question: Where is KFD found?
faqs.answer: KFD occurs primarily in the western ghats forest districts of Karnataka, India, particularly Uttar Kannada, Shimoga, Dakshina Kannada, and Udupi. It has spread to Kerala, Maharashtra, Goa, and Tamil Nadu.

faqs.question: How do people get KFD?
faqs.answer: KFD is acquired through the bite of infected Haemaphysalis spinigera ticks during forest activities. People can also be infected by contact with tissues of dead monkeys during KFD outbreaks.

faqs.question: When is the KFD transmission season?
faqs.answer: KFD transmission occurs from November to June, with peak activity between January and March when Haemaphysalis ticks are most active.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of KFD?
faqs.answer: KFD causes sudden high fever, severe myalgia, retro-orbital headache, vomiting, and diarrhoea in the first week. In the second week, some patients develop haemorrhagic manifestations including petechiae, bruising, nosebleeds, and gastrointestinal bleeding.

faqs.question: Is KFD fatal?
faqs.answer: The case fatality rate of KFD is 3-10% in hospitalised patients. Most patients recover with supportive care; deaths occur in the haemorrhagic phase from multi-organ failure and uncontrolled bleeding.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for KFD?
faqs.answer: There is no specific antiviral treatment for KFD. Management is supportive: fluid management, blood product transfusion for bleeding, and ICU care for severe cases.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for KFD?
faqs.answer: Yes. An inactivated KFDV vaccine is available in endemic areas of Karnataka. Two doses given 4-6 weeks apart provide 70-80% protection. Annual boosters are recommended for at-risk populations.

faqs.question: Who should get the KFD vaccine?
faqs.answer: People living and working in endemic KFD districts of Karnataka, particularly those who enter forests regularly, should receive the vaccine. It is offered free through Karnataka state health department campaigns.

faqs.question: How is KFD diagnosed?
faqs.answer: KFD is diagnosed by RT-PCR for KFDV RNA in the first week of illness, or by IgM ELISA from day 5-7 onwards. Platelet counts, liver function tests, and coagulation studies guide severity assessment.

faqs.question: What is the connection between monkey deaths and KFD?
faqs.answer: Monkey deaths (particularly Hanuman langurs and bonnet macaques) are sentinels for KFD activity. When monkeys die in a forest area, it signals that KFDV is circulating in ticks and human cases usually follow within 1-2 weeks.

faqs.question: Can you get KFD from another person?
faqs.answer: No. KFD is not transmitted person-to-person. The virus is maintained in a tick-mammal cycle. Humans are incidental dead-end hosts.

faqs.question: How do you prevent KFD?
faqs.answer: Prevention includes the KFDV vaccine, wearing protective clothing (long sleeves, trousers tucked into socks), applying DEET tick repellent, performing daily tick checks, and avoiding contact with dead monkeys in endemic areas.

faqs.question: How long does KFD last?
faqs.answer: The initial febrile phase lasts 1-2 weeks. Haemorrhagic manifestations typically resolve within 1-2 weeks with care. Full recovery takes 4-8 weeks for most patients.

faqs.question: What is the difference between KFD and CCHF?
faqs.answer: Both are tick-borne viral haemorrhagic fevers. KFD is caused by a flavivirus transmitted by Haemaphysalis spinigera ticks in Karnataka forests. CCHF is caused by a nairovirus transmitted by Hyalomma ticks in different regions and has a higher fatality rate.

faqs.question: Is KFD related to dengue?
faqs.answer: KFD and dengue both cause fever and haemorrhagic manifestations in India, and they co-circulate in Karnataka during the same season. However, they are transmitted by different vectors (ticks vs. mosquitoes), have different geographic patterns (forest vs. urban), and are confirmed by different laboratory tests.

faqs.question: Can you die from KFD?
faqs.answer: Yes. The case fatality rate is 3-10% in hospitalised patients. Deaths are caused by massive haemorrhage, multi-organ failure, or secondary infections in the haemorrhagic phase.

faqs.question: How quickly do KFD symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: The incubation period of KFD is 3-8 days after a tick bite. Symptoms typically begin with sudden high fever, headache, and severe body pain.

faqs.question: Is KFD related to the Ebola virus?
faqs.answer: No. KFD is a flavivirus (related to yellow fever and dengue viruses) while Ebola is a filovirus. They are unrelated and belong to different viral families with different geographic distributions and transmission patterns.

faqs.question: What should I do if I find a tick on me in a KFD area?
faqs.answer: Remove the tick immediately with fine-tipped tweezers, grasping the tick close to the skin. Clean the bite site with antiseptic. Monitor for fever in the next 2 weeks and seek medical care if fever develops, mentioning the tick bite.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for KFD?
faqs.answer: No specific cure exists for KFD. Treatment is supportive: managing fever, bleeding, and organ failure in hospital. Recovery depends on the body's immune response and quality of supportive care.
